data.title: Vision 2030: How Mining Could Power Saudi Arabia's New Economy
data.description:
As Saudi Arabia accelerates its economic diversification under Vision 2030, the mining sector is emerging as a cornerstone of the Kingdom's new growth model. Ambitious reforms, increased exploration budgets and a strategic focus on gold and copper are positioning Saudi Arabia to become a global mining leader. However, the sector's rapid expansion faces challenges from resource constraints, workforce development and heightened environmental, social and governance expectations.

The take

– Saudi Arabia's minesite exploration budget increased to $146 million in 2025 from $21 million in 2022.

– In 2025, 72% of the total exploration budget went to gold and 23% to copper, reflecting the Kingdom's evolving priorities and global market demand.

– The Kingdom hosts 24 exploration and 17 advanced projects, with copper and gold reserves expected to last 15 and 40 years, respectively, amid challenges in maintaining and expanding these reserves.

– The number of projects drilled in Saudi Arabia increased to 160 in 2024 from 58 in 2023, driven by Vision 2030 initiatives, regulatory reforms, heightened investor interest and improved infrastructure.

– Saudi Arabia is in the final implementation stage of launching a metals exchange to support the mining sector with financial tools, price stability and integration with global commodity markets.

Sector transformation and strategic focus

Saudi Arabia is experiencing a transformative shift in its mining sector, propelled by its Mining Strategy — a key pillar of the Kingdom's Vision 2030 aimed at diversifying its economy and reducing reliance on oil. Substantial increases in exploration budgets, particularly for gold and copper, reflect the Kingdom's strategic priorities and ambition to unlock new mineral resources. Supported by advanced technologies and a robust pipeline of projects, Saudi Arabia is positioning itself as an emerging leader in global mining, fostering sustainable growth and attracting significant foreign investment. The Kingdom's mineral wealth is now estimated at over $2.4 trillion, up 90% since 2016, following comprehensive surveys that have revealed new discoveries beyond traditional mineral belts, including rare earth elements and battery metals.

Exploration budget trends

Saudi Arabia's commitment to expanding its mining sector is evident in the substantial year-over-year increases in exploration budgets since 2022. The minesite exploration budget surged 595%, reaching $146 million in 2025 from $21 million in 2022. This growth is driven by ongoing geological surveys, technological advancements and higher exploration budgets, all of which signal stability and opportunity, attracting foreign investment. While the growth is striking, exploration spending remains modest compared to established mining jurisdictions such as Australia and Canada, which typically allocate over $1 billion annually.

Commodity focus and market dynamics

The allocation of exploration budgets by commodity has shifted significantly, reflecting Saudi Arabia's evolving priorities and market dynamics. Since 2022, the share allocated to gold has risen sharply, commanding approximately 72% of the total exploration budget in 2025. This underscores gold's importance as a driver of sector growth, foreign investment and economic diversification. Copper accounted for 23% of the 2025 budget, recognizing its rising global demand, especially for energy transition and infrastructure development. By prioritizing gold and copper, Saudi Arabia is positioning itself to capitalize on future market opportunities and drive long-term growth. Other commodities, such as silver, potash and lanthanides, still attract investment, but their relative share has declined, driven by promising new deposits and strategic responses to international market trends. Saudi Arabia aims to enter the top 20 for copper- and gold-producing countries globally.

Project pipeline and regulatory reforms

Minesite exploration accounted for 62% of the 2025 exploration budget, with these funds going toward expanding existing operations, increasing resource estimates and supporting near-term production growth. Focusing spending on minesite activities shows an emphasis on yielding quick results. It also allows reserves near ongoing operations to be brought into production more rapidly.

Saudi Arabia hosts a robust pipeline of 24 exploration and 17 advanced projects. Its proactive approach to geological surveys and resource assessment has uncovered significant potential across gold, copper, phosphate and bauxite. Substantial government investment and advanced technologies support this project pipeline. Additionally, a new mining investment law has reduced the tax rate to 20% from 45%, enhancing investor confidence and aligning regulations with global standards, which accelerates mineral development in line with Vision 2030. In its ninth licensing round, Saudi Arabia awarded 172 mining sites across three mineral-rich belts, attracting about 671 million Saudi Arabian riyals (US$179 million) in pledged investments and marking the largest licensing round in its history. The 10th round, covering 13,000 sq km, will be announced at the Future Minerals Forum in Riyadh on Jan. 13–15, 2026.

Mineral reserves and project activity

The Kingdom's mining sector is underpinned by substantial mineral reserves, with copper estimated at 15 years of reserve life and gold at 40 years, providing long-term security for ongoing operations and enhancing investor attractiveness. A lengthy gold reserve life enhances Saudi Arabia's role as a key player in the global gold industry.

In 2024, Saudi Arabia experienced a marked increase in drilling activity, with the number of projects rising to 160 from 58 in 2023. Vision 2030 targets increasing mining's GDP contribution to 240 billion Saudi Arabian riyals (US$64 billion) by 2030, creating 200,000 direct and indirect jobs, and attracting US$27 billion in new investment. Key policies include the new mining investment law, streamlined permitting, enhanced geological surveys and incentives for foreign investors. These measures are designed to foster a competitive, transparent and sustainable mining industry. The country is also leveraging its mineral wealth to develop downstream processing capacity and secure feedstock for green industrial projects, both domestically and through strategic investments globally, such as in Africa.

Ma'aden and international partnerships

State-owned Saudi Arabian Mining Co. (Ma'aden) is central to Saudi Arabia's mining ambitions, leading major projects and joint ventures. Key operations include the Jabal Sayid copper mine in Madina with Barrick Mining Corp.; the Al Ba'itha bauxite mine in Al Qassim province and the Ras Al Khair aluminum complex in Ash Sharqīyah (the Eastern province) with Alcoa Corp.; and large-scale phosphate mining at the Wa'ad Al Shamal mine in Al Ḩudūd ash Shamālīyah with The Mosaic Co. While Ma'aden and its established partners remain dominant, interest from Asia, Europe and North America is growing, with companies exploring partnerships, technology transfer and investment opportunities in gold, copper and industrial minerals. These international players are drawn by Saudi Arabia's resource potential, supportive regulatory reforms and the strategic importance of the Arabian Shield. A mineral-rich geological region in western Saudi Arabia, the Arabian Shield is known for its ancient rocks and significant deposits of gold, copper and other metals. Ma'aden has also established a joint venture with Hancock Prospecting Pty Ltd. to accelerate exploration in the Nabita Ad-Duwayhi Gold-Belt, covering over 24,000 sq km, further strengthening international collaboration and exploration efforts.

Challenges and outlook

Despite significant regulatory reforms that have elevated Saudi Arabia's position in the global mining industry, ongoing improvements in transparency and governance are essential to meet international standards. Sustained success will depend on overcoming several ESG challenges. The Arabian Shield, central to Vision 2030's diversification strategy, remains underexplored. High exploration spending does not always guarantee discoveries due to geological complexity, limited historical data and technical challenges. Operational risks are heightened by water scarcity and infrastructure gaps in remote mining regions, requiring substantial investment in desalination, recycling and the development of roads, power and communications. Additionally, workforce shortages and logistical issues could slow project timelines. Price volatility and the need for robust financial tools are being addressed through the new metals exchange, which aims to provide stability and attract global expertise. Ultimately, maintaining momentum and attracting investment will hinge on responsible resource management and effective ESG practices, as global investors increasingly prioritize environmental stewardship, workforce safety and community engagement.

As Saudi Arabia continues to implement reforms and attract global investment, the mining sector is poised for substantial growth. Overcoming ESG challenges will be critical to realizing Vision 2030's ambitions and establishing the Kingdom as a global mining leader. With the launch of the metals exchange, expanded licensing and high-profile events like the Future Minerals Forum, Saudi Arabia is advancing its vision to become a hub for mineral trading, processing and responsible supply chains — both regionally and globally.

data.title: Royal Bank Of Canada: It's Still A Dividend Play But Expensive
data.description:
It has been three months since I previously covered Royal Bank of Canada (RY). Its value has already risen by 15% despite my cautious stance. As a new investor, I am thankful that I decided to follow my plan to buy some of its stocks and test it for a while. I understand the optimistic market sentiments considering its robust performance recently. However, I still believe that this is not yet the right time to disregard downside risks as upside potential becomes narrower. Valuation is already high today despite some flaws in my previous article. Technicals are still bullish, but recent overbuying and insider selling may warrant some waiting time to find a better entry point.

RY Q4 2025: A Solid Ending Amid Macroeconomic Volatility

The macroeconomic conditions in the US and Canada have improved in the past three months. However, volatility is still present due to stubborn inflation and tariff concerns. As a bank, Royal Bank of Canada is still susceptible to headwinds due to its sensitivity to economic changes and cyclical nature. Even so, it continues to demonstrate its fundamental strength and prove why it’s one of the Canadian Big Five Banks. In its most recent performance, we saw how it navigated a volatile market environment with ease.

In Q4 2025, its interest income, the primary revenue component of RY, remained stable and flat on a YoY and QoQ basis. It amounted to CAD 26.3B, down by 0.8% YoY from CAD 26.5B. Yet, it increased by 0.7% from my previous coverage at CAD 26.1B. If we look at it on a YoY basis, it is easy to point out that the policy easing cycle impacted the yields of its interest-earning assets. It is most visible in loan interest, which decreased by 1.5%. After all, lower interest rates mean lower interest income on variable-rate loans.

On a lighter note, the interest rate cut raised loan demand. Both retail and whole loans rose. Overall, the loan base increased YoY and QoQ. This allowed the company to stay resilient as it regained its strength from Q3 to Q4 2025. Also, it benefited from higher yields on securities. Moreover, lower rates enticed more banking customers, so RY enjoyed higher commissions and fees. So, its non-interest income increased and completely offset interest income. Lastly, lower interest rates lowered the interest on deposits and borrowings. As a result, the total revenue reached CAD 17.2B, up by 14.2% YoY from CAD 15.1B and by 1.4% from CAD 17.0B in my previous coverage.

Risk Factors

RY is a solid bank if you ask me. But we should still know that it’s not completely shielded from various headwinds. Inflation may still impact the repayment capacity of borrowers. Tariff woes may exacerbate this since the extra costs incurred by domestic producers may be passed on to consumers. This may lead to higher prices in the market and affect the finances of businesses and consumers. So, delinquencies and defaults are also possible.

Mitigants

Despite the potential risks, I believe that RY is still well-positioned. As we can see, it benefits from the recent policy easing cycle. The move by the Fed may somehow offset the impact of inflation. Why? Lower interest rates increase borrower and investor confidence. This is why its loan base increased last quarter. Also, lower rates boost economic activity, which may raise employment and wages. As a result, consumers and borrowers may still keep up with the rising prices. This may also allow them to repay borrowings on time.

Moreover, RY maintains its strategic fundamental positioning to cushion the impact of macroeconomic headwinds and protect its business in case of a crisis.

One essential consideration here is its well-diversified assets. Like the other Big Five banks I’ve recently covered, RY does not solely rely on loans for its capital base and revenues. In fact, it maintains the balance between growth and stability. As you can see, only 53% of its interest-earning assets come from loans. To be honest, I think this is much lower than a typical percentage of 70%, 80%, or 90% since it’s a bank. This shows that it has multiple revenue streams.

Meanwhile, investment securities comprise 29%. The remaining 18% comes from its deposits in other banks and securities purchased under reverse repurchase agreements. I like this diversification strategy because it allows RY to be flexible with growth and risks. It also spreads risks because the weakness in one interest-earning asset can be offset by another. If you look at its income statement, interest income on investment securities rose by CAD 883M. This completely offset the decrease in interest on loans and deposits.

Its loan base is also well diversified. Its wholesale loans cover various sectors, allowing it to be exposed to multiple opportunities. Note that some sectors are not very sensitive to inflation and tariffs. These may include oil and gas, government, public works and infrastructure, and telecommunication. We should also note that wholesale loans are mainly for large projects by governments and businesses. More or less, there is little to no exposure to consumer loans. Meanwhile, its consumer loan exposure in the retail segment is minimal. In retail loans, personal loans and credit cards only comprise 22% of the total. If we add the wholesale loans, the actual consumer loan exposure is only 14%.

Now, why do I think it matters? First, businesses and government are more resilient and resistant to inflation. They are highly capable of repaying borrowings on time. Second, business loans are typically secured and collateralized, unlike credit card loans. Considering this, it should not be surprising that its credit quality in Canada remains stable and decent. Total delinquencies are still low at 0.40% and the same as in Q4 2024 and Q3 2025.

Lastly, its loan split between fixed and variable rates is strategic. For instance, only 35% of its Canadian Residential Mortgage Portfolio has variable rates. The remaining 65% of these loans have fixed rates. This allows RY to limit the unfavorable impact of the recent rate cut. Only that portion is subject to lower repricing rates. This can be another reason why the interest income on loans remains manageable despite the rate cut by the Fed and BoC.

And for its capital adequacy, RY remains well capitalized. Its 13.5% CET1 Ratio shows that its highest-quality capital can cover that portion of risk-weighted assets. If I add the other Tier 1 capital, the ratio will be 15.1%. And if I add the remaining capital, the Total Capital Ratio will reach 16.8%. This is higher YoY and QoQ. This is also more than twice the Basel III requirement of 8.0%. With that, it is safe to assume that RY has enough capital buffer. It can survive a recession and remain liquid without resorting to borrowing. It can also stay operational even if deposit runs happen. This is supported by its low Loan-To-Deposit Ratio of 69%. This proves that RY has excess reserves since it does not use more than 30% of deposits for loans. Also, the remaining interest-earning assets, like deposits and investment securities, are liquid.

Valuation

As I revisited its valuation, I realized some flaws in my previous coverage.

First, price ratios show potential stock overvaluation as RY trades above its five-year average. P/E shows that the stock trades at 16.87x its EPS versus the average of 13.79x. If I use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $138.29. The same applies to P/B and P/S, which suggest TPs of $132.60 and $133.24, respectively.

However, this method does not always assure us of the price direction. After all, price ratios only reveal whether the stock is cheaper or more expensive than its historical levels. The market may not care about it that much. And as long as the EPS/revenue/book value increases, the price will most likely follow. The same happened to the stock price of RY.

Nonetheless, we cannot deny the fact that the stock is already a bit too high for a buy now. Risks must still be considered. With that, I still believe that buying at this price is not reasonable.

For a more forward-looking analysis, I also used the Dividend Discount Model, or DDM. The annualized dividend is now $4.73 versus $4.39 in my previous coverage. So, the growth rate is now 6.27% based on the six-year CAGR on Seeking Alpha. I still set its risk premium at 5.0% to be realistic. Lastly, I set its Beta at 1.00, assuming the stock to be in line with the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, my new TP is $161.43. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $153.36.

Now, why did my DDM TP increase? First, the dividend growth rate increased from 6.0% in my previous coverage to 6.27%. Second, Beta decreased from 1.04 to 1.00 in line with the S&P 500. Third, the cost of equity decreased to 9.20% due to the decrease in Beta. The new TP gives logic to the recent price rally. However, I am expecting some downside risks since the price has already exceeded my new TP.

Technicals

RY’s technicals are still bullish as the price increases with higher highs and lows. It moves above the 50-day and 200-day SMA, showing that uptrend continuation is still possible. Why? The current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA also trends upwards, suggesting that market sentiments are still optimistic.

But MACD and RSI show some downside risks. The MACD line is now trending downwards below the signal line. This demonstrates decreasing buying volume and/or increasing selling volume. The histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume in the past week has already outpaced buying volume. RSI adheres to this as it shows overbuying. This may lead to a dip due to profit-taking. With that, I advise investors to wait on the sidelines as we await profit-takers to move and set new support.

Conclusion

Royal Bank of Canada remains a solid banking stock with robust fundamentals and strategic asset diversification. It is well-positioned against macroeconomic headwinds due to its high capital adequacy and excess reserves. However, valuation is still too high for my appetite and appears quite unreasonable today. Technicals adhere to it as the stock becomes overbought, which may lead to profit-taking and a dip. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: U.S. Export Coal Extends Rally To Close Out 2025
data.description:
US coal market prices edged higher in December, extending a steady rally dating back to October. The gains to year-end 2025 accrued mainly to export benchmarks.

Through 2027, firm natural gas prices are expected to support increased coal generation, driving stable or growing coal production. After 2027, the US coal market is forecast to face pressure from the expansion of zero-carbon electricity, incentivized in part by broad-based tax credits. Overall, the S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast projects 38.4 GW of coal plant retirements by 2035, with much of the planned retirement activity deferred until after 2030.

Coal plant generation share through 2035 is forecast to decline slowly to just 7.5% from 18.7% of total generation in 2026. The rapid phaseout of tax credits under the recently approved US tax and spending bill — also referred to as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" — is forecast to slow wind and solar generation growth, allowing coal plants to run more often after 2030. The most production uplift will take place at the Powder River Basin (PRB), followed by the Illinois Basin (ILB), as the reduced growth of wind and solar generation mainly affects the generation mix in the Midcontinental US.

The take

  • US coal prices again moved higher in December, even as production and shipments trended essentially flat year over year for the month.
  • The S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast anticipates 38.4 GW of coal plant retirements by 2035, amid market pressure to retain existing generation due to greater reliability needs and slower forecast growth in solar and wind energy.
  • The production outlook varies by region, with the Powder River Basin and Illinois Basin expecting stable to slightly growing production through 2027, while Appalachian coal production is forecast to decline, due to reduced domestic demand and limited export growth.

Coal prices clawed higher in December, with export benchmarks showing the most gains. CAPP region export benchmarks added $1.00/short ton to $82.00/short ton (1.2%), NYMEX CAPP added $2.00/short ton (2.6%) to $80.00/short ton, and NAPP Pittsburgh Seam 13,000 Btu per pound held flat at $62.00/short ton. Illinois Basin 11,500 mid-sulfur moved $0.50/short ton higher to $51.75/short ton, while the NYMEX Powder River Basin benchmark held at $15.00/short ton.

The cold snap driving heating demand across the eastern US in late November mostly abated by mid-December. Henry Hub spot gas prices held mostly firm, opening December at $4.59/MMBtu and falling to a low of $3.31/MMBtu over the holiday season before rallying to close the month at $4.40/MMBtu. Spot prices averaged $4.28/MMBtu for the month. Storage withdrawals continued strong through mid-December, with working gas at 3,579 Bcf as of Dec. 12 — 32 Bcf above the five-year average and 61 Bcf below the same week of 2024.

Except for the Northeast US, regional gas market prices were subdued. Chicago Gate averaged $3.91/MMBtu, a $0.37/MMBtu discount to Henry Hub. TCO Pool's discount averaged $0.44/MMBtu for a monthly average of $3.84/MMBtu, while TETCO M3 went to a premium of $1.00/MMBtu for a monthly average of $5.28/MMBtu. SoCal Border averaged $3.20/MMBtu during the month, $1.08/MMBtu below Henry Hub.

The US Energy Information Administration (EIA) estimated September 2025 coal stockpiles at 105 million short tons, essentially unchanged from August. The mild summer and associated weak coal demand provided some cushioning against subsequent drawdowns during November-December.

Current forward pricing for PRB coal is flat and stable, reflecting sufficient inventories at power plants and mining capacity to increase production as needed against firmer natural gas prices. After 2027, lower natural gas prices and declining coal demand are forecast to restrain price growth.

Bituminous coal price levels are primarily influenced by export markets, with today's price levels making domestic coal generation generally less competitive against Northeast natural gas. Firmer eastern natural gas prices nevertheless boosted eastern coal demand in the first half of 2025, mitigating a decline in Asia-Pacific seaborne coal demand compared to 2024..

Pricing benchmarks exceeding $65/short ton suggest sustainable returns for eastern bituminous coal, with Atlantic Basin exports above that threshold and coal competing in Pacific Basin export markets generally below. After declining in 2024, bituminous coal demand for electric generation is expected to remain stable through 2027 on higher electricity demand and more supportive natural gas prices. Declines in steam coal demand are expected to resume after 2027, and overall eastern US coal demand is forecast to decline 47 MMst in 2025–30.

Outlook for US coal production demand

For the four weeks ending Dec. 27, coal shipments averaged 9.9 MMst, 1.6% above 2024 levels. This continues the fourth-quarter trend of elevated inventories keeping shipments in line with 2024, following elevated shipments during the first half of 2025. Shipments ended 2025 at an aggregate 535 MMst, implying an inventory drawdown from year-end 2024 of 5-10 MMst.

The chart below compares the current production forecast with recent history. We forecast increased coal demand against higher natural gas prices through 2027. Gas-to-coal switching during the first quarter of 2025 reduced coal inventory surpluses from 2024-end, setting up production growth in 2025. We now forecast 2025 coal production at 530 MMst, an increase of 18 MMst (3.4%) from 2024 levels. Coal generation is forecast to further gain market share from natural gas through 2027, until relative coal and gas pricing normalizes and expanding green energy again puts pressure on coal generation. The overall coal market, comprising domestic demand and exports, is forecast to decline by 141 MMst between 2025 and 2030.

Production outlook — Powder River Basin

Production reports of the Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA) through the third quarter of 2025 indicate production of 172.2 MMst, an annualized rate of 229.6 MMst. Production is now forecast at 230 MMst in 2025, with higher inventories potentially constraining fourth-quarter production. Production is forecast to grow through 2027 against higher natural gas prices. By 2030, S&P Global Energy projects that coal retirements in the Midwest and expansion of wind generation in PRB's core markets will gradually shrink the coal demand to 192 MMst, declining further to 156 MMst through 2035.

Production outlook — Illinois Basin

MSHA's September quarter 2025 production reports indicate production of 50.6 MMst, or an annualized rate of 67.5 MMst. We forecast annual production will grow to 78 MMst through 2027, after which the expansion of wind generation and announced coal retirements are forecast to erode ILB coal demand. Coal production in the ILB is forecast to fall to 60 MMst by 2030, declining further to 39 MMst by 2035.

Production outlook — Appalachian basins

MSHA's production reports for the year's third quarter indicate year-to-date production at 118.8 MMst, or an annualized rate of 158.4 MMst. Appalachian coal demand tends to be more sensitive to global seaborne markets; PRB and ILB tend to be driven by domestic natural gas prices. As the latter two are forecast to experience improved demand versus natural gas generation, gains in Appalachian coal will be more limited. We forecast 2025 production at 159 MMst, just 0.6% higher than 2024. As remaining domestic demand erodes after 2027, with only modest offsets from export growth, Appalachian production is forecast to fall to 112 MMst by 2030.

Further information

Market indicative coal forecasts by S&P Global Energy represent forward curves for spot-traded instruments, analogous to a strip of contracts. The shorter tenors — current year and prompt year, plus additional years, if available — are driven by the observed/assessed market. The longer tenors — typically forecast years three to 20 for physically assessed markers — are driven by fundamental estimates of cash costs of production, accepted returns to capital, regional productive capacity, and forecast supply and demand. For the long-tenured portion of the curve, S&P Global Energy forecasts prices for specific coal markers and defines the remaining markers via historical spreads.

data.title: Vista Energy: Strong Buy Confirmed After Analyst Day
data.description:
Intro

More than two months ago, in late October 2025, my last analysis about Vista Energy (VIST) was published. As a reminder, Vista is a Mexican-based energy producer that specializes in unconventional energy production in the Vaca Muerta (“dead cow” in English) shale field in Argentina. The main product is oil.

The stock is practically still trading where it was at the time of my last analysis.

However, this does not mean that the stock is a boring non-mover; to the contrary. During the year 2025, shares got almost cut in half from high to low as a result of falling energy prices but also local elections in Argentina. Prices are still low, but the political uncertainty is gone, at least for now.

In November 2025, Vista Energy held its latest investor day to update shareholders on the new future goals. After the big acquisition of Petronas Argentina, the company has become significantly bigger, changing the operating dynamics.

In this update, I am discussing the latest changes and the newly issued guidance, reiterating my strong buy vote for VIST stock.

The Dead Cow is Alive—Scaling to Success

Vista Energy hosted its third Investor Day on 12 November 2025, highlighting remarkable progress since its inception in 2018 and unveiling ambitious self-funded growth targets. Here is the conference transcript, and here are the presentation slides.

Here is the long-term chart for the company.

Stepping into the CMD, co-founders and executives, including CEO Miguel Galuccio, presented how Vista has tripled production and quadrupled adjusted EBITDA since 2021 (which was practically rock-bottom year for Argentina and its equities). The executive team has positioned Vista as a benchmark in Vaca Muerta shale with industry-leading efficiency.

The good news is, despite the big leap forward, this growth story is far from over. Expansion plans show another almost double in expected production by 2030. Given that the management in the past has guided rather conservatively, delivering frequent beat-and-raise results, I am viewing this new target as achievable.

The key differentiator from now on will be that Vista (finally) gave a firm FCF target. This has been missing so far, as Vista focussed on volume growth, together with rising sales and EBITDA, while FCF practically all the time was flowing around zero only—the result of relentlessly reinvesting every dollar earned back into the business for aggressive growth. Having a beacon for true shareholder profitability in sight accordingly is welcome.

From 2021 to 2025, total production (oil and gas, measured in barrels of oil equivalent per day) surged from 39,000 boe/d to 114,000 boe/d. At the same time, adjusted EBITDA climbed from $380 million to approximately $1.6 billion, or by 4x. To the right of the slide below, we can see what is meant by “industry-leading”—profitability metrics, despite rather unambitious energy, especially oil, prices, come in strong. Of every barrel sold, Vista “nets” back $40 (at oil prices of around $60 only), while the return on average capital employed is 31%.

It is rather obvious, but this is in times of very LOW energy prices.

Just for comparison, here is the EBITDA margin of Vista (orange) versus Big Oil behemoths Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX)—which are worlds apart. Of course, the businesses are not 100% equal, as Vista has no refinery business, but just as a broad yardstick to show why I like the company so much—it is extremely profitable on an operational basis AND growing like weeds at the same time.

After a few reforms and undoing of strict interventions of the predecessor government, Vista now holds the top spot as Argentina's largest oil exporter, driven by a clearly much more business-friendly environment. The fact that Vista has grown into the country’s largest oil exporter highlights that we are not talking about just a small fish in an exotic jurisdiction, but a company that needs to be taken seriously.

Exports now comprise 62% of oil production (up 10 percentage points since 2023). At the same time, Vista has expanded its well inventory by over 500 locations to 1,653, with 1,300 yet to be drilled. In essence, their runway is huge, despite big early success. Production costs before taxes and royalties are just $11 per barrel of oil equivalent.

To reduce operational risks, management secured four drilling rigs, two frac sets, almost doubled oil treatment capacity, and vastly extended pipeline capacity since 2023 (to first reduce and later entirely quit costlier transports via trucks), enabling resilient operations while cutting friction risks.

Well productivity consistently outperforms Vaca Muerta peers as well as Permian (the biggest US shale field) averages by a wide margin.

Looking ahead, Vista's 2026-2028 plan targets production at 180,000 boe/d by 2028—or a 58% increase from 2025—with adjusted EBITDA reaching $2.8 billion (+75%). In other words, the growth story is in full swing, supported by strong output growth, while scaling into an even more profitable business.

As a result, the cumulative free cash flow over this three-year period is guided to be $1.5 billion, providing flexibility to return cash to shareholders—while strongly growing in parallel.

Annual CapEx of $1.5-1.6 billion will support tying in 80-90 wells yearly, generating the $1.5 billion in cumulative FCF at Brent prices of $65/bbl in 2026 and $70/bbl thereafter. Given these prices are a smidgen above the current Brent price, which is trading for $63 at the time of writing.

This does not worry me at this stage, though, as Vista has outperformed and overdelivered in the past. A bit lower prices could be balanced out by higher productivity achievements and/or higher volumes, an option the “big guys” do not have, meaning they are much more reliant on commodity prices—an external factor that is outside the control of the companies. Likewise, I do not believe that oil prices will crash and stay at a low level, as rig count has been dropping in the US shale business over the last year.

Exports are projected by management to drive 75% of revenues by 2028, growing each year from the current 60% base and diversifying to global markets while providing a natural hedge.

By now, Vista has also reached international price parity (IPP), meaning the company is not coping with artificially and politically suppressed prices it is allowed to use, but what the market is providing. Should oil prices surprise to the upside, Vista will be fully on board.

The 2030 plan—it sounds far away, but it is just four years—envisions over 200,000 boe/d production and $1.5 billion annual recurring free cash flow at mid-cycle $70/bbl Brent, underscoring Vista's transformation into a low-cost and cash-generating powerhouse. So, between now and 2028, $1.5 billion on a cumulative basis, and by 2030, on an annual basis. These are clear figures to operate with, which I am happy management provided us with.

Also important, the balance sheet. Vista took on some debt to finance the Petronas acquisition in 2025. The good news, management is targeting a significantly lower leverage ratio below 1x by 2028 from the current 1.5x. Debt maturities are well spread over many years, with no single year showing a huge wall of debt. FCF is expected to grow to $200 million, $500 million, and $800 million over the next years until the end of FY 2028, providing enough self-generated liquidity post-Capex.

Finally, capital allocation prioritizes share buybacks, debt reduction, and selective Vaca Muerta M&A. We will see how that turns out concretely, but for now, my key takeaways are that growth ambitions are defined and self-financed.

Vista Energy currently has a market cap of $5 billion, net debt of $2.6 billion, and an enterprise value of ~$7.6 billion. With $1.5 billion in future FCF (cumulative over the next three years, on an annual basis by 2030) in a low-price oil environment, this stock is not expensive. Using these figures, we arrive at an EV/FCF of 5x or an FCF yield of 20%. In my view, this reads very attractively, given that the oil price is low. Especially, and even more so, if debt gets paid back, there’s no need for much higher oil prices to justify the current share price. In this case, either the EV falls for an even lower multiple, or, and this is what I am expecting, the equity portion of the EV could see a re-rating to the upside. Ideally, supported by buybacks, as I have not heard any signs about a dividend being planned.

So, a 20% FCF yield today in a low-price environment for a company that tends to underpromise and overdeliver is a good starting point.

In my opinion, this is a phenomenal risk and reward setup, justifying a strong buy rating.

Risks to My Thesis

A sustained crash in oil prices below $50 per barrel—not my expected favorite scenario, but not ruled out entirely—could erode netbacks and force CapEx cuts, delaying production and FCF targets. Geopolitical instability or political/regulatory changes in Argentina might disrupt export flows and increase operational costs, potentially throwing the company noticeably back from its goals. Failure to achieve planned cost reductions in drilling and completion could strain free cash flow generation and leverage ratios.

Conclusion

Vista Energy's derisked, export-driven growth strategy, backed by superior operational metrics and financial resilience, positions it for substantial value creation, warranting a strong buy rating.

data.title: Precious Metals Define Commodity Performance In 2025
data.description:
In 2025, gold and silver drove commodity returns. Looking ahead, structural supply dynamics, geopolitical developments, and global demand trends remain key factors influencing commodity markets.

Market Overview

Commodity performance in both the fourth quarter and full year 2025 was largely driven by precious metals, particularly gold and silver, with returns concentrated in a limited number of commodities. Year-to-date, the UBS CM Commodity Index (“CMCITR”) returned 9.5%, while the Bloomberg Commodity Index (“BCOM”) returned 15.8%. CMCITR performance was supported by gains in metals and livestock, despite weakness in energy and agriculture. Differences in performance primarily reflected variations in sector composition—most notably CMCITR’s lower structural exposure to precious metals—rather than broad-based weakness across the commodity complex.

A defining feature of CMCITR is its greater allocation to industrial metals and reduced allocation to precious metals relative to broader commodity benchmarks. In 2025, this positioning influenced relative outcomes, as precious metals accounted for a significant share of overall commodity returns.

The chart below highlights the differing allocations to precious metals and industrial metals between CMCITR and BCOM.

Comparative Index Sector Weights

Sector Performance Summary

Sector-level returns highlight the divergence across commodity markets during the year:

  • Precious metals rose 74.7%, representing the largest positive contribution to CMCITR returns. Gold gained approximately 63%, while silver rose roughly 139%, marking historically strong annual performance for both metals.
  • Industrial metals increased 28.6%, supported by strong gains in copper and aluminum.
  • Livestock gained 25.2%, driven primarily by higher live cattle prices.
  • Energy declined 5.9%, reflecting persistent oversupply conditions.
  • Agriculture fell 4.1%, with weakness across several major crops.

Despite headwinds in energy and agriculture, gains in metals and livestock supported a positive full-year outcome for the index.

Precious Metals: Concentrated Source of Returns

Precious metals’ performance was supported by a combination of factors, including geopolitical uncertainty, sustained central-bank demand, and reserve diversification trends. A roughly 9% decline in the U.S. dollar provided additional support. Trade policy uncertainty, ongoing conflicts in the Middle East and Ukraine, and continued central bank purchases contributed to elevated demand. During the second half of the year, investor participation increased, with notable inflows into gold, silver, and mining-related investment vehicles.

Industrial Metals: Relative Strength

Industrial metals were the strongest-performing sector outside of precious metals, supported by supply constraints and rising expectations for demand related to electrification and data center infrastructure. Copper led the sector with a gain of approximately 44%, while aluminum rose about 20%.

Livestock: Tight Supply Conditions

Livestock prices contributed positively, led by live cattle, which rose roughly 30%. U.S. cattle inventories remain near multi-decade lows, and herd rebuilding is expected to occur gradually, influencing supply conditions.

Energy Markets: Supply-Driven Pressures Persist

Energy markets were a consistent drag on performance. Crude oil prices remained under pressure amid ample global supply following OPEC production increases. Both Brent and WTI crude declined by approximately 9%, while natural gas fell about 11%. Supply conditions suggest energy markets remain well balanced, with near-term price impacts uncertain.

Agriculture: Mixed but Generally Weak

Agricultural markets produced mixed results but trended lower overall. Cocoa and sugar recorded the largest declines, while soybeans finished modestly higher. Corn prices declined, reflecting favorable supply conditions.

Outlook for 2026

Looking ahead, commodities may continue to play an important role in diversified portfolios. Structural supply dynamics, geopolitical developments, and global demand trends remain key factors influencing commodity markets. While price volatility is likely to persist, commodities may offer diversification characteristics within broader asset allocation frameworks.

data.title: Emerson Electric's Comeback: A 'Boring' Business Setting Up For Solid Long-Term Returns
data.description:
With Emerson Electric Co. (EMR) being a rather boring business, it is not regularly showing up on our radar or occurring in news stories daily. It is rather a “buy-and-forget” business, and it has also been a year since I published my last article about the company and the stock. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

Emerson Electric performed well in the last 2.5 years since my previous article was published. But that performance is in part built on the expensive of valuation multiples – making the stock just more expensive and therefore not such a good investment anymore.

In my last article I rated the stock as a “Buy” – in this article I will rather rate Emerson Electric as a “Hold” again. I would not argue that the stock is a bad investment and Emerson Electric is a stable and good company. But in my opinion, we can expect at best an annual return in the high single digits in the next few years and there might be better investments out there.

And in the last year, Emerson Electric has not been a bad investment. When including the dividend, Emerson Electric slightly outperformed the S&P 500 over the timeframe. In the following article, I will answer the question of whether Emerson Electric will continue to perform more or less in line with the S&P 500 or whether Emerson Electric actually is a “Buy” now. In the article I will argue why I still think Emerson Electric has a wide economic moat around its business (despite some metrics struggling) and why we might be more optimistic about Emerson Electric in the years to come—making the stock a “Buy.” However, we start by looking at the last annual results, which were good results.

Annual Results Fiscal 2025

Emerson Electric increased sales from $17,492 million in fiscal 2024 to $18,016 million in fiscal 2025, resulting in 3.0% year-over-year growth. And while the company grew its top line only in the low single digits, operating income increased 45.2% year-over-year from $2,020 million in fiscal 2024 to $2,934 million in fiscal 2025. Diluted earnings per share also increased from $3.43 in the previous year to $4.04 in this year, resulting in 17.8% year-over-year growth. Adjusted earnings per share also increased year-over-year, but only 9.3%, from $5.49 in fiscal 2024 to $6.00 in fiscal 2025.

Finally, free cash flow—one of the most important metrics for any business—increased from $2,898 million in fiscal 2024 to $3,245 million in fiscal 2025, resulting in 12.0% year-over-year growth.

Emerson Electric reports in two different segments. Intelligent Devices increased sales 2% year-over-year to $12,400 million in fiscal 2025. Software and Control increased 5% year-over-year to $5,691 million in fiscal 2025.

Wide Economic Moat

Before we look at the growth potential of Emerson Electric in the years to come and finally answer the question of whether Emerson Electric is a good investment at this point, we focus on the economic moat. In my first article about Emerson Electric (published in April 2021), I argued for a wide economic moat around the business, which was built on three potential sources: the intangible assets the business has (including patents and the brand name), the switching costs as well as cost advantages.

When looking at the return on invested capital in the last two years, we actually have to question whether Emerson Electric has a wide economic moat. I often use the threshold of 10% RoIC to determine whether a company might have a wide economic moat (and of course it is never that simple). And in fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2025, the company reported “only” an RoIC of 7% to 8%.

On the other hand, the return on invested capital was 17.24% in the last ten years and 19.91% in the last five years. Additionally, management was pointing out during the last Investor Day that Emerson Electric will focus on improving the return profile again, and cash return on invested capital mostly declined due to portfolio transformation.

Additionally, Emerson Electric was able to improve its margins in the last few years, which is also a good sign for a business that has pricing power and most likely a wide economic moat around its business.

Emerson Electric is probably not a business we associate with constant outperformance, but when including dividends, an investment in Emerson Electric would have beaten the S&P 500 over the long run. Of course, the outperformance is nothing compared to other companies like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), but the Alpha metrics are quite solid. In the last five years, Emerson Electric generated an Alpha of 1.12; over the last ten years, the Alpha was 1.25, and over the last 30 years, Alpha was 1.42 (a number of 1.00 indicates a performance exactly in line with the S&P 500).

Summing up, I would still argue that Emerson Electric has a wide economic moat around its business, although the company was struggling a little bit in the last few years (at least when looking at fundamentals). An economic moat is also helping a company to protect its business against competitors, but it does not necessarily lead to high growth rates. In the next question, we therefore look at the important question of whether Emerson Electric can continue to grow at a high pace.

Continued Growth

Emerson Electric certainly reported solid results for fiscal 2025, and management is also optimistic for fiscal 2026. Net sales are expected to grow around 5.5% compared to fiscal 2025 (and underlying sales growth is expected to be 4%). Adjusted earnings per share are expected to be between $6.35 and $6.55, which is resulting in about 6% to 9% bottom-line growth. And finally, free cash flow is expected to be in a range of $3.5 billion to $3.6 billion—resulting in growth rates of 8% to 11% year-over-year.

Aside from the guidance for fiscal 2026, management also offered growth assumptions for the next three years until 2028 during its Investor Day in November 2025. During the presentation, CFO Mike Baughman presented several key assumptions for the next three years and targets for fiscal 2028 by focusing on 5 key metrics:

I'll take you through the plan in a little bit more detail than you've seen, and there'll be lots of numbers, as you would expect, but there's going to be 5 key numbers that you've heard before that represent our targets for 2028, and that includes $21 billion of revenues, 30% adjusted segment EBITDA margins, $8 of adjusted earnings per share, a cumulative $12 billion of free cash flow over that 3-year period, and we plan to land 2028 at a 20% free cash flow margin. So those are the 5 key numbers. And I think there's an important bonus number to talk about that a lot of investors, I think, will appreciate. which is the $10 billion that we plan to return to shareholders in the form of increased dividends and share repurchases over the next 3 years.

When looking at these numbers, we can only call them very optimistic. Revenue would grow with a CAGR around 5.2%, and a cumulative free cash flow of $12 billion would result in about $4.25 billion in free cash flow in fiscal 2027 and fiscal 2028 (as management is expecting about $3.5 billion in free cash flow for fiscal 2026). And most impressive among the different assumptions is, in my opinion, the free cash flow margin of 20%, which is targeted for fiscal 2028 (but we will get back to this later).

Management is also planning to spend about $6 billion in share repurchases during these three years. And while the company is planning to use only about $1 billion for repurchase in 2026, it has to spend about $2.5 billion in fiscal 2027 and 2028 to reach that target. Taking the current market capitalization of $81 billion, Emerson Electric can repurchase almost 7.5% of its shares outstanding, which would contribute about 2.5% to bottom-line growth annually.

In the last 30 years, share buybacks have always been a part of Emerson Electric’s capital allocation strategy. Since the late 1990s (the earliest data I have here), the number of diluted shares outstanding declined from 892 million to 565.5 million today. This is resulting in a CAGR of 1.6% over the last almost three decades. Hence it seems like Emerson Electric is accelerating its share buybacks.

Emerson’s Value Creation Framework is also very optimistic for high growth rates over the long run. Organic sales growth is expected to be between 4% and 7%, and with the combination of share buybacks as well as margin improvements, management is expecting adjusted earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 10%.

The question to answer is how realistic these growth assumptions actually are. When looking back at the past few years, Emerson did not really report extremely high growth rates but was rather struggling.

For starters, management is pointing out the $85 billion addressable market for Software & Systems, which is growing in the mid-single digits, and the $75 billion market for Intelligent Devices, which might also grow in the mid-single digits in the years to come. In both markets, Emerson Electric is claiming to have market leadership in about 40% to 45% of served markets. And we can assume that Emerson Electric will be able to grow in line with the overall market, and therefore mid-single-digit growth would be possible without taking market share from competitors.

Management is also betting on secular tailwinds due to investments of several trillions in sectors like power, life sciences, semiconductors, and aerospace & defense. In total, these five key growth verticals are expected to grow in the high single digits.

The huge advantage for Emerson Electric and us investors is the rather high level of predictability for this business. Emerson Electric’s revenue is not built on some new market or ongoing major investment cycle (like the AI infrastructure that is currently being built out). This is making revenue much more predictable, and we can raise the probability in our favor that management’s predictions about the overall market and revenue growth are correct.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

A final step in our analysis is the calculation of an intrinsic value for the stock. We can start by looking at simple valuation multiples, and at the time of writing, Emerson Electric is trading for 36 times earnings as well as 31 times free cash flow. And although the current P/FCF ratio is even slightly below the 10-year average, we certainly have to question whether Emerson Electric is a good investment when trading for such high valuation multiples.

But as always, I will use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock, as it is much more precise and can account for different growth rates in the years to come. We calculate with a 10% discount rate, as this is the annual return we like to achieve over time. Additionally, we are calculating with 573.9 million in diluted shares outstanding, and as a basis for our calculation, we use the midpoint of the free cash flow guidance for fiscal 2026 ($3.55 billion). Similar to my last article, I will assume 8% growth for the next ten years, followed by 4% growth into perpetuity. When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $134.48 for Emerson Electric, making the stock still slightly overvalued.

We can—and maybe should—be a little more optimistic. We assume a free cash flow of $4.0 billion (for fiscal 2027) and $4.5 billion (for fiscal 2028) and assume 8% growth for the years to come. Additionally, I would assume 5% growth instead till perpetuity, as Emerson Electric has a wide economic moat around its business (see section above), and management is expecting 10% adjusted EPS growth over the long run. When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $164.42 for Emerson Electric, and the stock would be slightly undervalued.

Conclusion

In my opinion, we can see Emerson Electric being slightly undervalued at this point, and the stock might be a cautious “Buy.” However, that assessment is built on management being right about free cash flow, free cash flow margin, and long-term growth. If management is too optimistic in its assumptions, the stock is still fairly valued at best and rather a “Hold.” But as the CFO pointed out, Emerson Electric is being realistic with its targets:

So these are not aspirational targets for us at Emerson. These are the result of our rigorous planning process, and this is what we're going to be laser-focused on as we move forward.

We could also argue to wait for another drawdown, as Emerson Electric has declined about 20% or more on several occasions, and a correction for the stock market (and Emerson Electric) is not unlikely at this point.

data.title: Ciena: A Stock To Benefit From AI Infrastructure Buildout
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am rating Ciena Corporation (CIEN) a strong Buy because the market still treats optical networking like an old telco spend. All this while, management is describing something much closer to an AI-era capacity land grab. They just put up a $1.35Bn quarter, beat normalized [EPS] by $0.14, and delivered a $60.93Mn revenue beat. The most important signal is that Ciena is guiding to a step change year with fiscal 2026 revenue of $ 5.7Bn to $6.1Bn and an adjusted operating margin target of about 17%. And that's a very different profile from the steady single-digit narrative that hangs around a lot of networking names. And this makes the chess analogy here quite simple. GPUs are the queen, flashy and decisive. But the network is the rook that controls the open files and decides whether the queen can even get to the fight.

The key point is that AI spending doesn't end at the GPU rack. The data still has to move, and the economics only show up when the workload leaves the data center and turns into a product. Ciena's CEO put it bluntly on the earnings call:

...traffic needs to leave the data center to be monetized and operationalized...

And that mattered because it reframes Ciena as part of the AI commercialization layer, not just the plumbing behind it. It also fits with the operating reality that hyperscalers keep repeating in different words. They're constrained by power, but they're also constrained by network architecture and optic capacity. And this is especially as AI inference gets distributed and training clusters get more geographically spread out. So, that's the setup of why CIEN's order book has started to look less cyclical and more structural.

My thesis is that Ciena is becoming a high-speed connectivity compounder because AI is forcing a rebuild of wide area networking, data center interconnect, and coherent optics at the same time. According to McKinsey & Company, estimate more than $7Tn of capital spending through the end of the decade in AI-related infrastructure. Of course I am not taking that number as the gospel. But I'm taking it as context for why a company that sells the bandwidth layer can see multiyear demand without needing to guess which model wins, which GPU wins, or which cloud app becomes the next runaway hit. I believe if you buy the view that AI is a decade-long spend cycle, then the bandwidth layer doesn't get a 'nice-to-have budget.' It gets a 'make it work or nothing else matters' budget, which is a very different kind of demand. And yes, that often comes with chunky quarters and messy mixes, but that's why the debate exists and why the opportunity is here.

Also, when I look at CIEN's metrics, the quantamental overlay supports that setup. Cien's growth metrics show that the company is sitting at 19.92% FWD revenue growth vs. an 8.14% sector median. This is a 144% relative gap, as shown below. The same table shows a 43.45% FWD EBITDA growth versus 12.06% sector median and 55.83% FWD diluted EPS growth. This is against the 13.62% sector median. And I would say that this is not a mature networking vendor kind of math. But rather it is a 'earning power stepping up' kind of math. The market can keep arguing about whether the multiple is too high, but if the earnings line keeps moving that fast, then the valuation debate turns into a timing debate.

Business Overview

Ciena is an optical and networking systems company that sells the hardware, software, and services used to move huge amounts of data across metro, long-haul and submarine networks. This is to mean that it builds the high-capacity pipes and the control software that let cloud providers and service providers push traffic efficiently. In the Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation, the company splits the business into networking platforms, global services, and Blue Planet automation. The customer base spans cloud providers, telecom operators, cable operators, and public sector-style deployments, and management has been explicit that cloud is now a major growth engine. And this matters because cloud demand tends to come in large, programmatic waves, which can look lumpy q/q but create very strong multi-quarter visibility once the program starts. And in the current AI cycle, that's exactly the kind of revenue you want to see in your funnel.

Growth Drivers

The catalyst for Ciena is not a single product launch. But rather, it is the way AI changes where bandwidth bottlenecks show up. Management framed cloud providers as being as focused on scaling their networks as they are on their access to power, which is about as direct as it gets. Power is the constraint everyone talks about, but network architecture is the constraint that decides whether that power turns into usable compute across geographies. CIEN specifically called out AI-driven use cases like scale across data centers, and those architectures are exactly where coherent optical transport is the limiting factor. The Q4 2025 report shows product and platform momentum across coherent optics, routing, and automation, including more advanced offerings that tend to show up in high-performance builds. When I combine that with guidance calling for a materially higher revenue year and higher operating margin, you get set up where demand and execution can reinforce each other. That's the loop you want in a growth compounder.

I also believe there is also a quieter catalyst here, which is that CIEN is investing ahead of demand instead of reacting late. For instance, the company management notes that results can vary q/q based on timing and mix, and this points to supply chain constraints, new product introductions, and global conditions as variables. In my own view, this reads like a company that understands the cycle it's in and wants to control the parts it can, especially capacity and product cadence. It's not glamorous, but I would say it's how you win in this category.

Valuation

Ciena's valuation looks expensive if you stop at the headline multiples, and that's why the stock still has skeptics. Ciena trades at about a FWD Non-GAAP P/E of 43x and a FWD EV/EBITDA of 28x. This is in comparison to 25x and 15x sector median, respectively. So for sure, those are real premiums, and they explain the D+ valuation grade. However, when I look at CIEN from a growth-adjusted perspective, I note that its FWD Non-GAAP PEG stands at 0.98x, while the sector median stands at 1.72x. This represents a 42% discount to the sector median on a growth-adjusted valuation. And when I look at CIEN on a GARP lens, then that PEG line is not a footnote but rather the whole argument. A stock can be expensive and still be mispriced if growth is accelerating faster than the market is willing to model.

Peer comps make the point clearer. Arista Networks (ANET), which investors often treat as an AI networking darling, trades at 42x FWD Non-GAAP P/E and 33x FWD EV/EBITDA. Yet the FWD Non-GAAP PEG is at 1.94x. This means that Ciena's FWD P/E is basically in line with Arista's, but its FWD PEG is roughly 50% lower and its EV/EBITDA is about 15% lower. Cisco Systems (CSCO), on the other hand, is an opposite comparison. CSCO looks cheaper at 17x FWD Non-GAAP P/E and 13x FWD EV/EBITDA, but its FWD Non-GAAP PEG is at 1.87x, which is almost double CIEN's 0.98. Lastly, Nokia (NOK) sits in the 'cheap but slow' bucket. And this is because it has a 21x FWD Non-GAAP P/E and 10x FWD EV/EBITDA, yet its FWD Non-GAAP reads at NM (Not meaningful). And this means that the growth math doesn't cooperate, as shown below. Now, none of that proves CIEN is cheap. It does support the idea that you are paying up for a growth profile that, on a PEG basis, is being valued like a mature name rather than like a fast grower.

Key Risks

The cleanest risk is that this cycle is still capex-driven, and capex can pause even when the long-term story stays intact. The earnings report on the outlook in itself is basically a risk checklist. It calls out the macro environment, the potential for deferrals, the timing of customer orders and shipments, and supply chain constraints as factors that can swing q/q results. If hyperscalers slow deployments for a couple of quarters because of power delays, permitting, or internal budget timing, CIEN can still print a messy sequence even if demand hasn't disappeared. Margin is the second risk. Ciena's management warns that gross margin can vary with product mix, pricing, and cost. Competitive intensity is the third risk because optical and routing markets never stay friendly for long, and big customers usually negotiate hard when volumes rise. Thus, I would say that the biggest risk in one line is a cloud capex pause that delays backlog conversion long enough for the multiple to compress before earnings catch up.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, I like Ciena because it's one of the cleaner ways to own the AI infrastructure buildout without needing to guess the winner at the model layer. CIEN's numbers and guidance point to a step-up year, and the growth metrics show forward growth rates that are multiples of the sector median. Valuation optics look uncomfortable on headline P/E and EV to EBITDA, but the FWD PEG is what keeps me constructive because it suggests the growth is not fully priced in. And I believe that if management executes anywhere near the 2026 margin and revenue targets, then the stock doesn't need multiple expansion to work. It can work by doing the boring thing, converting backlog, expanding margin, and letting EPS compound. And that's why I'm at Strong Buy rather than waiting for a pullback. The rook is already on the open file.

data.title: Western Asset Intermediate-Term Municipals Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: During the third quarter, volatility in both equity and Treasury markets remained relatively subdued. The U.S. Treasury yield curve bull-steepened, reflecting market expectations for slower growth, moderation in economic data and potential rate cuts. Risk assets performed well, with the S&P 500 Index reaching new highs and credit spreads tightening. Municipal supply conditions continue at a record pace as municipal issuance reached $155 billion in 3Q25, up 12% from 3Q24 levels. Tax-exempt issuance fell 2% quarter-over-quarter (QoQ) to $145 billion and taxable issuance fell 40% QoQ to $10 billion. Year-to-date new-issue supply of $439 billion is 18% higher than the prior year, with tax-exempt issuance up 19% year-over-year and taxable issuance in line with prior year levels.
  • Contributors: Overweight to the industrial revenue sector contributed to performance.
  • Detractors: Security selection in the leasing sector detracted from performance.
  • Outlook: Muni fundamentals remained resilient. While strong revenue collections are indicative of credit resiliency in the muni market, the pace of credit rating improvement from the three major rating agencies has slowed so far this year.

Performance Review

  • Duration positioning contributed to performance.
  • Overweight to the industrial revenue sector contributed to performance.
  • Security selection within the power sector contributed to performance.
  • Security selection in the leasing and local GO sectors detracted from performance.
  • Issue selection to IL securities detracted from performance.

Outlook

  • Western Asset believes longer maturities continue to offer attractive relative value for long-term investors, considering elevated absolute muni yield levels and rolldown opportunities from the curve which remains relatively steep versus taxable fixed income markets.
  • We anticipate that a strong labor market and consumer spending will support tax collections and municipal credit conditions over the medium term. However, we expect the potential for tax collections to be more critical for budgets if federal spending reductions extend more broadly to state and local budgets.

data.title: 2026 Is The Year Of The Stock Picker?
data.description:
By Anu Ganti

Recent market commentary has declared that 2025 was a brutal year for stock picking, with USD 1 trillion pulled out of active equity mutual funds during the year, according to the Investment Company Institute. The year was characterized by sharp double-digit swings for the S&P 500®, and in such environments filled with bouts of volatility, we would naturally have expected active managers to shine.

So what might explain this lackluster performance? One notable headwind, especially over the past three years, has been the outperformance of the S&P 500’s largest constituents. As a result, the weight of the top 10 stocks in the S&P 500® has climbed accordingly, from 31% in 2023 to almost 40% in 2025. If active managers were underweight the largest stocks, then they were more likely to underperform.

A related headwind for stock pickers was that these mega-cap gains were driven by a handful of stocks, leading to a positively skewed distribution of returns, with an average return greater than that of the median, consistent with 19 out of the prior 24 years. Consequently, only 30% of stocks outperformed the S&P 500 in 2025, slightly higher than in the prior two years but still relatively low by historical standards. If active managers held portfolios concentrated in a handful of stocks, they were more likely to miss out on holding the handful of winners.

Despite these obstacles, one silver lining may have been the rise in cross-sectional volatility, or dispersion, which measures how differently stocks are performing relative to each other. The value of stock-selection skill rises when dispersion is high, which could have meant greater opportunities for skillful stock pickers to outperform. Exhibit 3 shows that large-cap stock-level dispersion in 2025 was higher than the historical average and the prior two years. We know from our SPIVA® U.S. Mid-Year 2025 Scorecard that 46% of large-cap funds outperformed the S&P 500 in H1 2025,1 evidence that there were opportunities for stock pickers to shine, particularly during the tariff-related turmoil in the first half of the year.

As we begin 2026, we can look to implied dispersion to understand the potential for future opportunities for stock selection. We observe in Exhibit 4 that the Cboe S&P 500 Dispersion Index (DSPX), which uses listed options to measure the expectations for dispersion over the next 30 calendar days, rose to 33.81 as of Jan. 12, 2026. This means the market expects that the spread of annualized S&P 500 stock returns will have a standard deviation of 34% over the next month and, especially as we approach Q4 earnings season, could signify positive prospects. Another potential tailwind may be the recent broadening of the rally, with the S&P SmallCap 600® up 3% since the start of the year compared to the S&P 500.

The current environment may be fortuitous, but forecasting future active manager performance may be as or even more challenging than predicting future market performance. For those who have declared 2026 to be the year of the stock picker, a reminder that we heard similar proclamations at the end of 2024 and in many years prior, but as we know from our SPIVA Scorecards, majority underperformance remained the norm more often than not.

1 Please stay tuned for the SPIVA U.S. Year-End 2025 results.
